planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; wild ginger spreads slowly to form a dense, deer-resistant groundcover under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in deep shade to partial shade; wild ginger is one of the finest native groundcovers for dense shade, forming a low, tight carpet of large, velvety, heart-shaped leaves that suppresses weeds effectively under mature trees where even grass fails. The small, maroon-brown, jug-shaped flowers bloom at soil level in early spring, hidden beneath the foliage and pollinated by early ground beetles. The rhizomes have a spicy, ginger-like fragrance and have been used as a ginger substitute in traditional cooking, though they are not botanically related to culinary ginger (Zingiber officinale). An outstanding native groundcover for moist, deeply shaded sites under deciduous canopy.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; bloodroot is best moved in late summer after the foliage has died down, when the rhizomes can be divided and replanted without disturbing active growth.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; bloodroot is one of the most beloved eastern woodland spring ephemerals, producing large, pristine white flowers with golden stamens in very early spring — sometimes while snow still lingers — before the distinctive lobed, glaucous leaves fully expand. Each flower lasts only a few days, but the display is breathtaking in a naturalized setting; the double-flowered form 'Multiplex' holds its flowers longer and is especially prized. After blooming, the large, decorative foliage persists through summer before dying back completely. The rhizomes contain brilliant orange-red sap (sanguinarine) that is toxic; handle with gloves when dividing. A must-have for the native woodland garden.

planting.note: Direct-sow fresh seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; seeds require cold-moist stratification over winter for spring germination. Alternatively plant container-grown transplants in early spring.

directions: Direct-sow fresh seeds in fall or plant container transplants in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Virginia bluebells is perhaps the most spectacular native spring wildflower of eastern North America's floodplain forests, producing masses of nodding, trumpet-shaped flowers that open as pink buds and mature to sky-blue in mid-spring — the transformation from pink bud to blue flower is a memorable botanical curiosity. It naturalizes freely in moist, partially shaded woodland gardens, interplanted with hostas, ferns, or daylilies that fill the gap when bluebells die completely to the ground by early summer. It is a spring ephemeral — marking its location is important as all traces of it disappear by June. Outstanding for naturalizing under tall deciduous trees with dappled shade.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or fall in deep, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is slow to establish and may take 2–3 years to bloom from division, but is extremely long-lived once rooted.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or fall in deep, moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; black cohosh is a dramatic native woodland perennial that produces tall, wand-like spires of fragrant white flowers in midsummer that rise well above the large, compound foliage and glow in the dappled shade of a deciduous woodland garden. It has one of the longest histories of use in North American traditional medicine — the roots were used by many indigenous nations and are still widely sold as an herbal supplement for menopausal symptoms. Slow to establish but very long-lived; a well-placed clump will enlarge steadily over decades. The species was formerly classified as Cimicifuga racemosa; now merged into Actaea. Reclassified cultivars like 'Brunette' and 'James Compton' offer attractive dark-bronze foliage.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; mayapple spreads by rhizomes to form colonies and is most effective when planted in groups of at least five.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; mayapple is a distinctive native woodland plant of the eastern US that carpets the floor of moist deciduous forests with large, umbrella-like, deeply lobed leaves in spring. Only forked plants (bearing two leaves) flower, producing a single white flower hidden beneath the leaf canopy in late spring; the resulting lemon-sized, apple-like fruits ripen to yellow in late summer and are the only edible part — they have a sweet, tropical flavor used for jelly and drinks. All other parts of the plant are toxic. A dramatic and fast-spreading groundcover for moist woodland sites, naturalized meadow edges, and rain gardens under trees; populations can cover large areas within a few seasons.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil; wild columbine requires 4–8 weeks of cold-moist stratification and germinates readily the following spring. It self-seeds freely once established.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun, or start indoors with 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; wild columbine is one of the most important early hummingbird nectar flowers of eastern North America, producing nodding, lantern-shaped flowers with distinctive red-and-yellow spurred petals from mid-spring through early summer at exactly the time ruby-throated hummingbirds return from their winter migration. It self-seeds freely in suitable conditions to naturalize under deciduous trees, along rock outcrops, and in woodland garden edges. Short-lived (3–5 years) but perpetuated by abundant self-seeding that maintains a persistent colony. The lacy, blue-green, compound foliage is attractive from late winter through fall before dying back.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; sow fresh seeds immediately after harvest in fall, as they lose viability quickly when dried.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; jack-in-the-pulpit is one of the most architecturally distinctive native woodland wildflowers, with a hooded, striped, green-and-purple spathe (the 'pulpit') enclosing a club-shaped spadix ('Jack') in spring, followed by stalks of brilliant scarlet berries in late summer that are a critical food source for wood thrush and other forest birds. Plants change sex from year to year depending on corm size — small corms produce male flowers, while large, well-fed corms produce female flowers and thus berries. The corms and berries are toxic unless cooked; Native Americans dried and ground the corms extensively to neutralize the calcium oxalate crystals. A captivating and deeply native presence in the moist shade garden.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted softwood cuttings in spring or fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil; beautyberry performs equally well in sun or shade, though it tends toward looser form in deep shade.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; American beautyberry produces one of the most stunning fruiting displays in the native shrub palette — tight clusters of electric-violet purple berries encircling the stems from mid-fall through early winter, so vivid and remarkable that they stop visitors in their tracks. The berries are a critical food source for mockingbirds, catbirds, quail, robins, and dozens of other bird species, which strip the stems quickly. Cut the plant back hard in early spring before growth begins (it flowers and fruits on new wood) to maintain a compact, attractive form; without pruning it can become quite large and open. The crushed leaves are also a traditional mosquito repellent used by Creek and Cherokee peoples.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted softwood cuttings in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; sweetshrub thrives in the dappled shade at the edges of deciduous woodland.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; sweetshrub is an excellent native shrub for the woodland garden, producing unusual maroon-to-burgundy, many-petaled, strap-petaled flowers in late spring with a sweet, fruity, strawberry-spice fragrance that has been treasured for centuries — historically the dried flowers were placed in sachets and closets for perfuming linens. Fragrance intensity varies considerably among individuals, so select plants when in bloom or seek named fragrant cultivars like 'Athens' (buttery-yellow flowers) and 'Michael Lindsey'. The large, dark-green leaves turn butter-yellow in fall. Adaptable and low-maintenance once established; it suckers moderately to form a naturalistic clump.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; buttonbush is one of the very few woody shrubs that can grow with its roots submerged in standing water and thrives in areas that flood seasonally.

directions: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; buttonbush is an essential native shrub for wet, seasonally flooded, or permanently moist sites — pond margins, rain gardens, wet swales, and creek banks where it thrives in conditions that eliminate every other large shrub. The spherical, white, pincushion-like flower heads appear in midsummer and are one of the finest native pollinator plants available, attracting an extraordinary diversity of native bees, bumblebees, skippers, and swallowtail butterflies that few other native plants can match. The round, persistent seed heads provide winter food for waterfowl and songbirds. An outstanding choice for rain garden perimeters, bioswales, and naturalized wet areas in zone 5–10 landscapes.
